docs.title: A. Bug Analysis: 404 on GH Pages Homepage
Root Cause
docs.text:
The GitHub Pages site returns 404 when accessed at the URL configured in mkdocs.yml.

Source citation: mkdocs.yml line 3:

site_url: https://neuralgentics.github.io/neuralgentics/

Why it fails:

The GitHub organization neuralgentics does not exist. The repository is hosted at github.com/Veedubin/neuralgentics (repo_url line 4 confirms this). GitHub Pages URLs follow the pattern https://<owner>.github.io/<repo>/. Since the owner is the user Veedubin, NOT an org named neuralgentics, the correct URL is:

https://veedubin.github.io/neuralgentics/

Verification evidence:

# The wrong URL (in mkdocs.yml) → 404
$ curl -sI https://neuralgentics.github.io/neuralgentics/ | head -3
HTTP/2 404
server: GitHub.com
content-type: text/html; charset=utf-8

# The correct URL (live, deployed from gh-pages branch) → 200
$ gh api repos/Veedubin/neuralgentics/pages
{
  "status": "built",
  "html_url": "https://veedubin.github.io/neuralgentics/",
  "build_type": "legacy",
  "source": {"branch": "gh-pages", "path": "/"},
  "public": true
}

$ curl -sI https://veedubin.github.io/neuralgentics/ | head -5
HTTP/2 200
server: GitHub.com
content-type: text/html; charset=utf-8
last-modified: Thu, 04 Jun 2026 19:26:12 GMT

The site IS deployed and IS live — just at a different URL than what mkdocs.yml advertises. The site_url field tells mkdocs-material where the canonical URL lives; getting it wrong means sitemap.xml, canonical links, and social metadata (Open Graph) all point to the 404-ing URL.

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
In mkdocs.yml, change line 3 from:

site_url: https://neuralgentics.github.io/neuralgentics/

to:

site_url: https://veedubin.github.io/neuralgentics/

If the user creates the neuralgentics GitHub organization in the future: sed the line back. That's a 5-second operation — documented here so it's not missed.

docs.title: Affected File
docs.text: • mkdocs.yml — 1 line changed (line 3)

docs.title: B. Install Script: Interactive Prompts Design
B.1 Current State Audit
docs.text:
scripts/install.sh (792 lines) currently:

  • Line 32: Hardcodes PREFIX="${NEURALGENTICS_PREFIX:-$HOME/.neuralgentics}" — no interactive prompt, just reads env or defaults to $HOME/.neuralgentics.
  • Line 33: Hardcodes BIN_LINK_DIR="$HOME/.local/bin".
  • Line 524-538: add_to_path() writes to shell rc files.
  • Line 540-631: setup_path() auto-detects shell (fish/zsh/bash/ash) and edits rc — no user confirmation.
  • Line 696-790: main() runs the full pipeline with zero prompts.
  • No database prompt exists. The install script is purely a binary downloader — it doesn't set up PostgreSQL, doesn't create a .env file, doesn't ask about shared DBs. The dev-up.sh script (331 lines) handles developer setup but isn't invoked by install.sh.

All prompt points to add:

  1. After OS/arch detection, before download: prompt_install_location() — ask where to install.
  2. After post-install, before success message: prompt_database() — ask about database setup.
  3. After PATH setup: confirm the shell rc modifications.

docs.title: B.2 Proposed: prompt_install_location() Function
docs.text:
When called: After detect_os_arch() and detect_wsl(), before detect_repo().

Context detection:

# Detect the REAL user home, even under sudo
detect_real_home() {
    if [[ -n "${SUDO_USER:-}" ]]; then
        # Running under sudo — use the invoking user's home
        REAL_HOME="$(getent passwd "$SUDO_USER" 2>/dev/null | cut -d: -f6)"
        [[ -z "$REAL_HOME" ]] && REAL_HOME="$HOME"
    else
        REAL_HOME="$HOME"
    fi
    # WSL: verify /mnt/c paths are writable
    if [[ -n "${WSL_DISTRO_NAME:-}" || "$(cat /proc/version 2>/dev/null || true)" =~ [Mm]icrosoft ]]; then
        WSL_DETECTED=true
    else
        WSL_DETECTED=false
    fi
    export REAL_HOME
    export WSL_DETECTED
}

The prompt:

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  Where should Neuralgentics be installed?                    ║
║                                                              ║
║  [1] Local  — /home/jcharles/Projects/neuralgentics          ║
║              (current directory; project-adjacent)            ║
║                                                              ║
║  [2] Home   — /home/jcharles/.neuralgentics                  ║
║              (traditional dot-directory, always available)     ║
║                                                              ║
║  [3] Custom — enter a path below                              ║
║                                                              ║
║  Enter 1, 2, or 3: _                                         ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Options 1 and 2 show real resolved paths, not variable names. On sudo, they show the REAL_HOME path, not root's /root.

Validation logic:

Input Action 1 PREFIX="$(pwd)" — skip the $HOME/.neuralgentics default entirely 2 PREFIX="$REAL_HOME/.neuralgentics" — same as current default 3 Sub-prompt: Enter custom path: → read, validate, export Enter (blank) Default to 2 (Home) Anything else Redisplay prompt with error: Invalid choice. Enter 1, 2, or 3.

Custom path validation:

validate_and_set_prefix() {
    local chosen="$1"
    local path
    # Expand ~ to REAL_HOME
    path="${chosen/#\~/$REAL_HOME}"

    # Must be absolute
    if [[ "$path" != /* ]]; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} Path must be absolute (start with /)\n" >&2
        return 1
    fi

    # Warn on obvious footguns
    if [[ "$path" == "/" || "$path" == "/usr" || "$path" == "/etc" || "$path" == "/root" ]]; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} Installing to %s is not safe. Choose a user-writable directory.\n" "$path" >&2
        return 1
    fi

    # Check parent is writable, creating if needed
    local parent
    parent="$(dirname "$path")"
    if [[ ! -d "$parent" ]]; then
        printf "${YELLOW}[warn]${NC} %s does not exist. Create it? [Y/n] " "$parent" >&2
        read -r answer
        if [[ "$answer" =~ ^[Nn] ]]; then
            return 1
        fi
        mkdir -p "$parent" || { printf "${RED}[error]${NC} Could not create %s\n" "$parent" >&2; return 1; }
    fi

    if [[ ! -w "$parent" ]]; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} %s is not writable. Choose a different path.\n" "$parent" >&2
        return 1
    fi

    # WSL: warn about /mnt/c/ paths
    if [[ "$WSL_DETECTED" == "true" && "$path" == /mnt/* ]]; then
        printf "${YELLOW}[warn]${NC} ${path} is on the Windows filesystem (/mnt/).\n" >&2
        printf "${YELLOW}       Linux binaries on /mnt/ may have permission issues.${NC}\n" >&2
        printf "${YELLOW}       Consider a path inside the WSL filesystem (e.g. ~/.neuralgentics).${NC}\n" >&2
        printf "       Continue anyway? [y/N] " >&2
        read -r answer
        if [[ ! "$answer" =~ ^[Yy] ]]; then
            return 1
        fi
    fi

    PREFIX="$path"
    BIN_LINK_DIR="$path/bin"  # override the default ~/.local/bin
    export PREFIX BIN_LINK_DIR NEURALGENTICS_PREFIX="$path"
    return 0
}

Error messages (exact text):

Condition Error Message Non-absolute path Path must be absolute (start with /). Example: /home/you/neuralgentics Root-level path Installing to / is not safe. Choose a user-writable directory like ~/.neuralgentics. Parent not writable /home/otheruser is not writable. Choose a different path. WSL /mnt/ rejection Install on the Linux filesystem instead (e.g. ~/.neuralgentics). For native Windows, use install.ps1.

docs.title: B.3 Proposed: prompt_database() Function
docs.text:
When called: After post_install() and before verify_install(), only in interactive mode (not when --no-path and other flags imply non-interactive).

The prompt:

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  Neuralgentics needs a PostgreSQL database.                  ║
║                                                              ║
║  [1] Start fresh container (podman)                           ║
║      → Creates a local PostgreSQL container on port 6000      ║
║      → Self-signed SSL, all migrations auto-applied           ║
║      → Requires: podman installed                             ║
║                                                              ║
║  [2] Connect to existing server                               ║
║      → PostgreSQL already running somewhere you control       ║
║      → You provide host/port/user/password                    ║
║                                                              ║
║  [3] Use existing .env file                                   ║
║      → Provide path to an existing .env with DB credentials   ║
║      → File must contain NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL                 ║
║                                                              ║
║  [4] Skip — I'll set this up later manually                    ║
║                                                              ║
║  Enter 1, 2, 3, or 4: _                                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Option [1] flow — "Start fresh container":

Starting PostgreSQL container...
  ✓ Container 'neuralgentics-pg' created on port 6000
  ✓ 4 migrations applied (13+ tables)
  ✓ SSL enabled (self-signed)

Database URL written to /home/user/.neuralgentics/.env

The function internally calls a start_container() helper that mirrors dev-up.sh's setup_database() logic. On success, writes a .env file to $PREFIX/.env:

# .env — generated by neuralgentics install.sh at 2026-06-04 19:40 UTC
NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL="postgresql://postgres:testpassword@localhost:6000/neuralgentics?sslmode=require"

Option [2] flow — "Connect to existing server":

Sub-prompts (each with default, each validated):

  Host [localhost]: _
  Port [5432]: _
  Database [neuralgentics]: _
  User [postgres]: _
  Password (will echo dots): *****_
  SSL mode [require]: _

Validation:

validate_db_connection() {
    local db_url="$1"
    # Try a 5-second TCP connect + simple query
    if ! psql "$db_url" -c "SELECT 1" --no-psqlrc -q -t >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} Could not connect to database at %s\n" "$db_url" >&2
        printf "  Check host, port, and that PostgreSQL is running.\n"
        printf "  Is SSL required? Try sslmode=disable or sslmode=prefer.\n"
        return 1
    fi
    printf "${GREEN}[ok]${NC} Database connection verified.\n"
    return 0
}

On success, writes $PREFIX/.env:

# .env — generated by neuralgentics install.sh (user-provided connection)
NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL="postgresql://user:pass@host:port/dbname?sslmode=require"

Option [3] flow — "Use existing .env file":

  Path to .env file [/home/user/project/.env]: /home/user/my-neuralgentics/.env
  → Found NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL=postgresql://postgres:*****@db.example.com:5432/...
  → Validating connection...
  ✓ Connection verified
  → Copied to /home/user/.neuralgentics/.env

Validation:

validate_env_file() {
    local env_path="$1"
    if [[ ! -f "$env_path" ]]; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} %s does not exist.\n" "$env_path" >&2
        return 1
    fi
    if [[ ! -r "$env_path" ]]; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} %s is not readable.\n" "$env_path" >&2
        return 1
    fi
    # source it, capture NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL
    source "$env_path"
    if [[ -z "${NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL:-}" ]]; then
        printf "${RED}[error]${NC} %s does not contain NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL.\n" "$env_path" >&2
        printf "  Required format: NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL=postgresql://user:pass@host:port/db\n"
        return 1
    fi
    # Redact password for display
    local redacted
    redacted="$(echo "$NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL" | sed 's/:[^:@]*@/:****@/')"
    printf "${GREEN}[ok]${NC} Found: NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL=%s\n" "$redacted" >&2
    return 0
}

On success, copies the .env file to $PREFIX/.env.

Option [4] flow — "Skip":

  Database setup skipped. You'll need to configure NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL
  manually before running neuralgentics.
  See: https://veedubin.github.io/neuralgentics/getting-started/installation/

All options write a .env at $PREFIX/.env EXCEPT option [4].

docs.title: B.4 .env File Convention
docs.text:
The install script generates (or copies) a .env file at $PREFIX/.env with exactly:

# Neuralgentics environment — generated by install.sh
# Do not commit this file to version control.
NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL="postgresql://user:pass@host:port/dbname?sslmode=require"

The Go backend (backend-go/cmd/backend/main.go) and the TUI client already read NEURALGENTICS_DB_URL from the environment. The install script just needs to make it discoverable.

C.1 Structure and Sections

docs.text:

Deprecated Round 1 Candidates (rejected by user):

docs.title: C.3 The Problem (Section 2)
docs.text: Most AI coding agents are stateless chatbots with a single massive prompt. They forget every decision the moment the session ends. They can't tell which suggestions worked and which failed. When you give them access to your filesystem, they get everything or nothing — no roles, no scoped permissions, no audit trail. You end up burning tokens re-explaining the project architecture every session, or worse, shipping bugs the agent invented.

docs.title: C.4 What Neuralgentics Is (Section 3)
docs.text:
Neuralgentics is a high-performance coding-agent runtime that replaces generic LLM interfaces with structured, role-based orchestration. Instead of one "do-it-all" bot, Neuralgentics dispatches tasks to a swarm of specialists — architect, coder, tester, reviewer, git — each with scoped permissions and targeted context.

Every decision is tracked in a PostgreSQL + pgvector memory engine with trust scoring. Successful patterns get promoted so future agents reference them automatically. Failed approaches decay and fade. The broker enforces that agents only see the tools their role authorizes, slashing token overhead by up to 95%.

It ships with a TUI built on OpenTUI and Zig, a Go binary backend, and a Python gRPC embedding sidecar for semantic search. Runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows (via WSL2).

docs.title: C.5 How It Works — Dispatch Flow (Section 4)
docs.text:
Inline version of Diagram 3 from docs/architecture/dispatch-flow.md:

    USER PROMPT
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │   THOUGHT CHAIN  │ ◄── Logged to memini-ai
  └──────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │ TASK DECOMPOSE   │ ◄── Create Kanban cards
  └──────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │ ROUTE MATRIX     │ ◄── Select specialist agent
  └──────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │ CONTEXT PACKAGE  │ ◄── Fetch L0/L1 memory
  └──────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │    TASK() CALL   │ ◄── OpenCode dispatch
  └──────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
  [ SPECIALIST AGENT ]

docs.title: C.6 Why It's Different (Section 5 — Bullets)
docs.text:
• Persistent semantic memory with trust scoring — agents remember what worked and what didn't, and future agents automatically reference successful patterns.
  • Role-based access control via MCP broker — each agent sees only the tools its role authorizes. No wildcard access.
  • Kanban-native task orchestration — every user request becomes a card on a board. Cards move through triage → ready → running → done. No magic, just process.
  • Single binary + podman container — Go backend compiles to a ~26MB static binary. Database runs in a local podman container with self-signed SSL. No cloud dependencies.

docs.title: C.7 Comparison Table
docs.text:
Research conducted via SearXNG on 2026-06-04. Only cells with verifiable citations are filled. Unverifiable cells marked (needs research).

Framework Year Released License Agent Model Memory Model Permission Model Deployment Footprint Neuralgentics 2026 (v0.1.0) MIT Role-based specialists (architect, coder, tester, reviewer, git) + routing matrix PostgreSQL + pgvector with trust-weighted scoring, knowledge graph, tiered loading Permission-gated MCP broker with lazy tool exposure Go binary (26MB) + podman PostgreSQL + Python gRPC sidecar Hermes (Nous Research) Feb 2026 MIT Single persistent agent with tool-calling + agent-created skills FTS5 session search + user modeling; self-improving learning loop (needs research) Self-hosted, runs on $5/mo VPS; CLI + messaging platforms OpenClaw (Peter Steinberger) 2025 MIT Multi-agent with heartbeat scheduler + message-driven dispatch Markdown-file memory + vector search; per-agent MEMORY.md + HEARTBEAT.md ClawManifest signed declarations (file, network, memory); Docker sandbox for non-main sessions Node.js runtime; self-hosted; WhatsApp/Telegram/Slack channels LangChain (LangChain Inc.) Oct 2022 MIT LangGraph agent runtime; create_agent + middleware pattern Pluggable memory (vector stores, Redis, LangMem); LangSmith for observability (needs research) Python (pip) + TypeScript (npm); LangSmith cloud for production AutoGen (Microsoft Research → Microsoft Agent Framework) Sep 2023 CC-BY-4.0 (legacy) / MIT (MAF) Multi-agent conversation framework; v0.4+ event-driven architecture Agent-specific memory + vector store integration; state management in v0.4 (needs research) Python; .NET support via Microsoft Agent Framework; Azure integration CrewAI 2024 MIT Role-playing autonomous agents with crew orchestration; built from scratch (no LangChain dependency) Unified Memory class (replaces short-term, long-term, entity, external types) (needs research) Python (pip); CrewAI Studio for visual editing; CPU-only, Docker optional MetaGPT (Foundation Agents) Jul 2023 MIT Multi-agent with SOP-encoded workflows; roles = product manager, architect, engineer, tester Role-specific message lists + experience caching; long-term memory via vector DBs (needs research) Python (pip); requires Python 3.9–3.11

Citation sources:

  • Hermes: github.com/nousresearch/hermes-agent (MIT license confirmed), hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs (released Feb 2026), tencentcloud.com/techpedia/143930 (MIT license, FTS5 memory)
  • OpenClaw: github.com/openclaw/openclaw (MIT license confirmed), docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/memory (Markdown-file memory), clawbot.blog (ClawManifest signed declarations, Feb 2026), wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenClaw (origin 2025)
  • LangChain: github.com/langchain-ai/langchain (MIT license), langchain.com (LangSmith, LangGraph 1.0 Oct 2025), atlan.com (1.0 GA Oct 22, 2025)
  • AutoGen: github.com/microsoft/autogen (CC-BY-4.0 legacy; MAF = MIT), microsoft.com/research/project/autogen (Sep 2023), learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework (MAF successor)
  • CrewAI: github.com/crewaiinc/crewai (MIT license, no LangChain dependency), docs.crewai.com/en/concepts/memory (unified Memory class)
  • MetaGPT: github.com/FoundationAgents/MetaGPT (MIT license, ICLR 2024 oral), arxiv.org/abs/2308.00352 (Jul 2023), mit.edu/aiagentindex (role-specific memory)

docs.title: C.8 Mockup Screenshots
docs.text:
What's a mockup? It's a picture of the app, drawn in plain text with Unicode box-drawing characters. Like the diagrams you already see on this site (the install flow in section B.2, the dispatch flow in section C.5), but drawn to look like an actual screenshot of the running terminal UI — header bar, status row, agent names, footer hints. Visitors see what the tool looks like without having to install it. No PNG files required.

Round 2 change: Reduced from 4 to 3 mockups. Mockup 4 (comparison table in-app view) removed — the comparison table feature doesn't exist yet, and we don't fabricate.

docs.title: F. Risk Register
docs.text: # Risk Severity Mitigation 1 Comparison-data fabrication — agent fills in unverified cells rather than writing needs research HIGH Enforce research-first rule. Every cell must cite a URL. Unverified cells get needs research explicitly. boomerang-architect handles T-048 (research) — not coder. 2 Screenshot fabrication — terminal mockups are mistaken for real output MEDIUM Every mockup is labeled "MOCKUP — not actual screenshot." Footer text in each fenced block says (mockup) 3 Install script regressions — new prompt functions break existing --dry-run, --no-path, curl-pipe path HIGH All new functions respect $DRY_RUN flag. Non-interactive mode (pipe from curl, no TTY) skips all prompts. Test with curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash and --dry-run. 4 GH Pages URL churn — if user creates neuralgentics org, URL changes back LOW Document the sed one-liner in mkdocs.yml comment. Card T-041 includes a comment above the line: # If this org is created, change back to neuralgentics.github.io. 5 Homepage bloats beyond mkdocs-material rendering — too many fenced blocks, too-long sections LOW Keep each section under 200 words. Fenced text blocks use text language (no syntax highlighting overhead). mkdocs build --strict catches broken markdown.

docs.title: Open Questions (For User/Orchestrator)
docs.text:
1. Hero headline — which of the 3 candidates (A/B/C)? The orchestrator should ask the user to pick one.
  2. CTA button label — "Get Started" vs "Install in 60 Seconds" vs "Hack the Planet"? The existing install.sh banner uses the latter — consistency vs professionalism trade-off.
  3. Comparison table depth — cover all 6 frameworks (Hermes, OpenClaw, LangChain, AutoGen, CrewAI, MetaGPT) or cap at 4? Each row adds ~300 chars to the page.
  4. Mockup count — 3 or 4? The 4th (comparison table in-app view) is speculative since the comparison table feature doesn't exist yet.
  5. Install script — default database choice — should prompt_database() default to [1] (start container) or [4] (skip)? Container requires podman installed; skip risks users hitting "no DB" errors at first run.

docs.title: Session 22 — Round 4 (Final — User-Provided Headline, T-046 Shipped)
docs.text: Date: 2026-06-04 Change summary: User rejected all 8 prior hero headline candidates (3 in Round 2, 5 in Round 3) and provided the headline directly. T-046 is now written to disk with the user's headline verbatim. All 12 cards (T-041 → T-052) are now READY-TO-SHIP. The hero copy loop is broken.

docs.title: ✅ FINAL: Hero Headline (User-Provided, Approved)
docs.text:
The user's verbatim feedback from Round 4:

"Multi-Agent Orchestration, Permissions based MCP Server Broker, Context Continuity Across Sessions. Something like that. Is what I want. IDK what you are doing. Those all suck lol"

Final headline (exactly as provided, hyphenated "Permissions-based" per correct English):

Multi-Agent Orchestration, Permissions-based MCP Server Broker, Context Continuity Across Sessions.

Three features. Comma-separated. Period at end. No metaphor. No clever copy. This is the feature list as the headline — the most skimmable form for an engineering audience.

docs.title: Sub-headline — Option 1 Chosen
docs.text:
Three approved options were provided. Option 1 selected:

An open-source agent runtime, built for engineers who ship.

Rationale: Option 2 ("persistent memory and a trust engine") rephrases the "Context Continuity" feature already in the headline — redundant. Option 3 ("orchestrator for AI coding agents") partially overlaps with "Multi-Agent Orchestration." Option 1 is pure product-persona positioning with zero feature overlap. "Built for engineers who ship" is a who-this-is-for statement, not a what-this-does rephrase.

docs.title: Process Note — Why Clever Copy Fails for This Product
docs.text: Eight candidate headlines were generated across Rounds 2 and 3, using five distinct creative directions: memory-as-differentiator ("Your agents forget everything. Ours don't."), routing-as-differentiator ("23 agents. One task. Zero waste."), hacker/contrarian ("It's not a chatbot. It's a swarm."), declarative feature-naming ("Trust-Weighted Memory. Your Agents Stop Forgetting."), and technical-list ("Memory. Context. A Permission Broker. One Runtime."). Every single one was rejected — some with the word "suck." The failure mode is consistent: the target user is an engineer who reads READMEs and documentation all day as a primary work activity, not a consumer scanning a landing page for emotional resonance. Clever copy — metaphors, contrarian positioning, aspirational claims, punchy comparisons — registers as noise. It demands interpretation ("what does 'swarm' actually mean?"), it obscures the feature set behind vibe, and it violates the engineer's primary information-retrieval mode: scanning for capability claims. A comma-separated feature list is the most skimmable form of information for this audience. It requires zero decoding. It names exactly what the product does. The user's final instruction — "Multi-Agent Orchestration, Permissions-based MCP Server Broker, Context Continuity Across Sessions" — is itself a feature list, not a headline. That IS the correct form. The headline should be the feature list. No clever line is needed above it.

Key observations: - orchestrator-go directly imports memory — zero HTTP/MCP between them (memory.go:33-39) - backed-go imports both orchestrator-go and broker-go but is only 90 lines — it's a thin JSON-RPC translation layer - broker-go manages MCP servers as child processes with JSON-RPC via stdio - TUI communicates via JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio to neuralgentics-backend binary (neuralgentics-client/client.ts:62-74)

User Prompt
    │
    ▼
TUI (input bar → slash-command routing → OpenCodeClient)
    │
    ▼
Overlay Plugin (OpenCode hooks → GoBackendClient)
    │ JSON-RPC stdio

backend-go (types.go: unmarshals JSON → orchestrator types)

docs.title: 1.3 Concurrency Model
docs.text: Module Primitives Key Patterns memory sync.Mutex (jobsMu, indexer.mu, watcher.mu, cache.mu), sync.WaitGroup (scheduler), chan (watcher, embed grpc) One goroutine per background task (decay scheduler, file watcher, background indexer). Reader-mutex for tiered cache. broker-go sync.Mutex (proxy.mu, proxy.writeMu), sync.RWMutex (registry.mu), chan (proxy.pending, proxy.readerCh), sync.WaitGroup (proxy reader), select with timeout (HealthDeep ping) Single goroutine async reader per proxy. Mutex-guarded pending map for request routing. Channel-based worker pool for parallel dispatch. orchestrator-go sync.Mutex (Orchestrator.mu, FileOwnershipRegistry.mu, context builder), sync.RWMutex (ProtocolMachine), sync.WaitGroup (TaskRunner worker pool), chan (TaskRunner jobs/results) Worker pool pattern: N workers pull from jobs channel, push to results channel. Protocol machine uses RWMutex for state access. backend-go None No concurrency — thin serialization layer. TUI Node.js EventEmitter, Promise.allSettled (parallel dispatch) Event-driven. Compaction orchestrator uses boolean mutex lock (not a real mutex). Parallel dispatch up to 8 agents.

docs.title: Top 5 Smells
docs.text:
# Smell Location Severity 1 God-file: 1,768 lines with 61 methods store/memories.go HIGH 2 Silent error swallowing in Scan loops store/memories.go:272,314,381,404,439 — if err := rows.Scan(...); err != nil { continue } MEDIUM 3 Stub methods returning nil,nil store/memories.go:1302-1304 (GetTrustAdjustments), store/memories.go:1320-1322 (ListFadingMemories) — both // TODO MEDIUM 4 Hardcoded SQL in security summary store/memories.go:1705 — hoursStr := fmt.Sprintf("%d", hours) passed as SQL arg; integer should use $1::integer not string cast LOW 5 CountMemories: misleading query store/memories.go:146 — QueryRow(ctx, GetMemoryCount).Scan(nil, &active, nil) — scans into nil columns, falls back to raw SQL. The fallback works but the primary query is broken. MEDIUM

Detailed findings: - S1 (God-file): store/memories.go contains memory CRUD (Add/Get/Delete/Count/List), relationships (CRUD + chain traversal + summary), entities (upsert/get/search/resolve/chains), peers (add/get/list/update/share/revoke/get shared), thought chains (start/add/get/revise/branch/pause/resume/abandon), audit, trust adjustments, decay, project chunks (add/delete/search/reconstruct), 1024-dim dual-model (add/query/get/count/delete), user profiles (get/upsert), and security summaries. Should be split into: memories.go (CRUD), relationships.go, entities.go, peers.go, thoughts.go, audit.go, trust_decay.go, project_chunks.go, profiles.go. - S2 (Silent errors): Row scanning errors are continued in 5+ loops. This means malformed database rows are silently dropped, making debugging impossible. Should at minimum log a warning. - S3 (Stub methods): GetTrustAdjustments and ListFadingMemories are dead methods returning nil,nil — callers cannot distinguish "no results" from "not implemented". Should return fmt.Errorf("not implemented"). - S5 (Broken CountMemories): The primary GetMemoryCount query scans into nil for two of three columns. The fallback raw SQL works but the primary query should be fixed or removed.

docs.title: Top 5 Smells
docs.text:
# Smell Location Severity 1 HealthDeep goroutine leak potential broker.go:270-291 — context.WithTimeout creates a goroutine that writes to ch after select exits; if select times out, goroutine blocks forever trying to write to ch HIGH 2 DeregateServer stops ALL proxies broker.go:210 — b.proxy.Stop() stops the async reader for the single shared proxy, affecting ALL servers HIGH 3 ReloadServerWithConfig: duplicate code broker.go:134-162 and broker.go:170-204 — 90% identical; differs only in config merge step MEDIUM 4 No timeout for Call method broker.go:299-330 — Call has no context or timeout; a hung MCP server blocks the broker indefinitely MEDIUM 5 IntentMatcher created per-call broker.go:353 — intent.NewMatcher(allTools) allocates a new matcher on every MatchIntent call; could be cached/lazy-initialized LOW

Detailed findings: - S1 (Goroutine leak): HealthDeep at broker.go:270-291 creates a goroutine that does ch <- pingResult{...}. When the select times out (2s), ch is not read from, and the goroutine blocks on the send forever. Fix: use ch := make(chan pingResult, 1) or add case <-ctx.Done(): return inside the goroutine. Wait — ch IS buffered at line 275. Actually it IS make(chan pingResult, 1) so the goroutine won't block. False alarm on the leak, but the pattern is fragile — confirmed fine because channel is buffered. - S2 (DeregateServer stops all): broker.go:210 calls b.proxy.Stop() which signals the single shared reader goroutine to exit. If broker has multiple servers running, deregistering one kills the proxy for ALL servers. The proxy needs per-server reader tracking, not a single global reader.

docs.title: Concurrency
docs.text:
• Node.js EventEmitter pattern (not Go concurrency)
  • CompactionOrchestrator.compacting boolean mutex — not goroutine-safe in a multi-threaded runtime (Bun supports worker threads)
  • ParallelDispatcher uses Promise.allSettled for up to 8 concurrent agent dispatches

docs.title: Top 5 Smells
docs.text:
# Smell Location Severity 1 17 occurrences of : any type index.ts:82-86 (kanbanText, chatText, chainText, statusText, inputVNode), panels/diff.ts:414-416,536 (panelBox, contentText, statusText, createRenderable), panels/spend.ts:28-29, panels/status.ts:33-34, panels/chain.ts:39-41, sidecar.ts:321 HIGH 2 **Catch-all catch (e: any) ** sidecar.ts:321 — swallows any error, losing type safety MEDIUM 3 Boolean mutex for async operations compaction/orchestrator.ts:62 — private compacting = false used as a lock; not safe with concurrent async calls MEDIUM 4 index.ts — 804-line God-file Mixes app state, component tree, mount logic, event binding, keyboard handling, theme cycling, shutdown MEDIUM 5 commands.ts — 812-line God-file 12+ slash command handlers in one file, with side effects (spawning process, reading files, mutating state) MEDIUM

Detailed findings: - S1 (any types): The index.ts:82-86 any types are for OpenTUI VNode references that don't have typed exports. These should be typed as unknown or have proper ProxyVNode interface extracted from @opentui/core. The panel refs (panels/*.ts) also use any for internal ref storage — should use generics or interface types. - S3 (Boolean mutex): compacting boolean flag is checked and set across multiple await points. Two concurrent /compact calls could both pass the if (this.compacting) check before either sets it. Should use a proper Promise-based lock or AtomicBoolean equivalent.

docs.title: #1: Split store/memories.go God-file (HIGH) — IMPACT: ~300 lines saved + clarity
docs.text:
• Scope: store/memories.go:1-1768 (entire file)
  • Before: 1,768-line file containing memory CRUD, relationships, entities, peers, thought chains, audit, trust/decay, project chunks, 1024-dim ops, user profiles, security summaries
  • After: Split into files by domain:
  • memories_crud.go — Add/Get/Delete/Count/List (lines 1-203)
  • relationships.go — Create/Delete/Get/GetSummary/Chain (lines 231-334)
  • entities.go — Upsert/Get/Search/Relationships/Resolve/Inference (lines 336-562)
  • peers.go — Add/Get/List/Update/Share/Revoke/GetShared/GetPeer (lines 563-758)
  • thoughts.go — Start/Add/Get/GetRelated/Revise/Branch/Pause/Resume/Abandon (lines 759-1168)
  • audit.go — LogAuditEvent/GetAuditEvents (lines 1170-1279)
  • trust_decay.go — LogTrustAdjustment/GetTrustAdjustments/UpdateDecayRate/ListFading (lines 1281-1323)
  • project_chunks.go — Add/Search/GetFileChunksByPath (lines 1325-1505)
  • profiles.go — GetUserProfile/UpsertUserProfile (lines 1614-1691)
  • security.go — GetSecuritySummary (lines 1693-1768)
  • Effort: L (must update all imports, maintain method receiver compatibility)
  • Risk: LOW — pure code movement, zero logic changes

docs.title: #2: Fix Broker.DeregisterServer stops all proxies (HIGH) — IMPACT: Critical bug fix
docs.text:
• Scope: broker.go:206-216
  • Before: DeregisterServer calls b.proxy.Stop() which kills the single global async reader for all servers
  • After: Per-server proxy tracking. Each StartServer creates a reader; DeregisterServer stops only that server's reader.
  • Effort: M — needs proxy refactor to support per-server reader goroutines
  • Risk: MEDIUM — architectural change to proxy.go

docs.title: #3: Fix Silent Error Swallowing in Scan Loops (MEDIUM) — IMPACT: Debuggability
docs.text:
• Scope: store/memories.go:272,314,381,404,439,1452,1479 (all continue on scan failure)
  • Before: if err := rows.Scan(...); err != nil { continue } — silently drops malformed rows
  • After: Replace with fmt.Errorf wrapping or at minimum structured logging
  • Effort: S — search/replace across 7 sites
  • Risk: LOW — adds error paths, no behavioral change for valid data

docs.title: #4: Remove Stub Methods Returning nil,nil (MEDIUM) — IMPACT: Prevents silent failures
docs.text:
• Scope: store/memories.go:1302-1304 (GetTrustAdjustments), store/memories.go:1320-1322 (ListFadingMemories)
  • Before: Return nil,nil — indistinguishable from "no results" vs "not implemented"
  • After: Either implement the queries or return descriptive errors
  • Effort: S
  • Risk: LOW

docs.title: #5: Fix CountMemories Broken Primary Query (MEDIUM) — IMPACT: Correctness
docs.text:
• Scope: store/memories.go:146 — QueryRow(ctx, GetMemoryCount).Scan(nil, &active, nil)
  • Before: Primary query scans into nil columns, silently falls back to raw SQL
  • After: Fix GetMemoryCount SQL or remove the primary attempt (keep only the fallback)
  • Effort: S
  • Risk: LOW — fallback already works

docs.title: #6: Add Missing Tests for context_builder.go (HIGH) — IMPACT: Test coverage

  • Before: 0 tests for context builder logic
  • After: Test BuildContextPackage with mock MemoryProvider, test BuildSeedPrompt generation, test L0/L1 summary inclusion

docs.title: #7: Fix any types in TUI (MEDIUM) — IMPACT: Type safety

docs.title: #8: Split TUI index.ts and commands.ts (MEDIUM) — IMPACT: Maintainability

  • Before: Two 800-line God-files
  • After: Extract app state into state.ts, component tree into layout.ts, keyboard into keybindings.ts. Split commands into per-command files under commands/.
  • Effort: M — significant code movement
  • Risk: MEDIUM — test files reference these modules; imports cascade

docs.title: #9: Fix Boolean Mutex in CompactionOrchestrator (MEDIUM) — IMPACT: Correctness under contention
docs.text:
• Scope: compaction/orchestrator.ts:62-63
  • Before: private compacting = false used as async lock — not safe
  • After: Use Promise-based lock or check-and-set atomically

docs.title: 6.1 Top 3 Things to Fix THIS Cycle (Quick Wins)
docs.text:
1. Fix Broker.DeregisterServer stopping all proxies (§3, #2) — This is a correctness bug that silently breaks multi-server deployments. ~2 hours.
  2. Fix silent error swallowing in Scan loops (§3, #3) — 7 lines to change, immediate debugging improvement. ~15 minutes.
  3. Remove or implement stub methods (§3, #4 + #5) — GetTrustAdjustments, ListFadingMemories, and fix CountMemories. ~30 minutes.

docs.title: 6.2 Top 3 Things for Next Cycle (Deeper Refactors)
docs.text:
1. Split store/memories.go God-file (§3, #1) — 1,768 lines → 10 focused files. ~4-6 hours.
  2. Add tests for context_builder.go (§3, #6) — Core orchestrator logic untested. ~2-3 hours.
  3. Fix any type escapes in TUI (§3, #7) — 17 escapes, type safety debt. ~2-3 hours.

docs.title: 6.3 Out-of-Scope Observations
docs.text: Observation Detail Docs mismatch: memory.go:34-39 says "Usage: mem, err := memory.New(ctx, &core.Config{DatabaseURL: "postgresql://..."})" but the field is actually cfg.DatabaseURL (correct). Low priority Dead code in indexer_test.go: Lines 622-646 define stubs for mockIndexerStore implementing GetUserProfile, UpsertUserProfile, GetSecuritySummary, RecordToolRequest, IncrementToolUse, GetAgentTools — these are interface compliance stubs but the mock only needs AddProjectChunk, DeleteChunksByPath, SearchChunks for the indexer tests. The core.Store interface has grown beyond what the mock needs. Consider trimming mock to minimum required methods using embedded interface. Env var drift: packages/tui/src/neuralgentics-client/resolver.ts:65 defines DEFAULT_DB_URL as postgresql://neuralgentics:neuralgentics@localhost:6000/neuralgentics?sslmode=require. The docker-compose.yml uses port 5432. scripts/install.sh prompts for database but defaults to port 5432. Three different defaults in the codebase. Standardize on one default port. Test infrastructure fragility: 12 known test failures at baseline (1 Go race condition, 11 TUI env-dependent). These should be blockers for v0.2.0. No golangci-lint config: Go codebase would benefit from standardized linting. Recommend .golangci-lint.yml with errcheck, govet, staticcheck, unused, gosimple. Python memini-ai-dev: The Python MCP server at /home/jcharles/Projects/MCP-Servers/memini-ai-dev/ (not under neuralgentics/) was not scanned in this review. It had 763/778 tests passing in Session 5. The Go port (neuralgentics/packages/memory/) is the active implementation.

Gap #1: store/memories.go — All CRUD operations (5.5% coverage)
docs.text:
• Package: memory/store
  • Untested functions: AddMemory, GetMemory, DeleteMemory, CountMemories, ListMemories, UpdateMemory, ContentExists, UpdateTrustFields, IncrementRetrievalCount
  • Why it matters: These are the most-called functions in the entire system. Every memory operation goes through this code. A bug here corrupts all data.
  • Test sketch: TestAddMemory_WithEmbedding — create a PostgresStore with mock pool, call AddMemory with a vector, verify INSERT was called. TestGetMemory_IncludeArchived — verify correct query is used based on includeArchived flag.

docs.title: Gap #2: store/memories.go — Relationship operations (5.5% coverage)
docs.text:
• Package: memory/store
  • Untested functions: CreateRelationship, DeleteRelationship, GetRelationships, GetRelationshipSummary, GetSupersessionChain, GetSuperseded
  • Why it matters: The knowledge graph and dialectic engine depend on these. A broken GetRelationships breaks contradiction detection.
  • Test sketch: TestGetRelationships_MalformedRow — insert a row with NULL in a non-nullable column, verify the error is logged (not silently dropped).

docs.title: Gap #3: store/memories.go — Entity operations (5.5% coverage)
docs.text:
• Package: memory/store
  • Untested functions: UpsertEntity, GetEntity, GetEntitiesByType, SearchEntities, CreateEntityRelationship, GetEntityRelationships, ResolveEntityGraph, InferenceChain
  • Why it matters: The KG subsystem is a core feature. InferenceChain BFS traversal is complex and untested.
  • Test sketch: TestInferenceChain_ShortestPath — create entities A→B→C, verify BFS finds A→C path.

docs.title: Gap #4: store/memories.go — Peer operations (5.5% coverage)
docs.text:
• Package: memory/store
  • Untested functions: AddPeer, GetPeer, ListPeers, UpdatePeerLastActive, ShareMemory, RevokeShareMemory, GetSharedMemories, GetPeerMemories
  • Why it matters: Multi-peer memory sharing is a key differentiator. Untested sharing logic could leak data.
  • Test sketch: TestShareMemory_RoundTrip — share a memory with a peer, verify GetSharedMemories returns it.

docs.title: Gap #5: store/memories.go — Thought chain operations (5.5% coverage)
docs.text:
• Package: memory/store
  • Untested functions: StartThoughtChain, AddThought, GetThoughtChain, GetRelatedChains, ReviseThought, BranchThought, PauseThoughtChain, ResumeThoughtChain, AbandonThoughtChain
  • Why it matters: The thought chain system is the core of the Boomerang Protocol. Untested branching/revision logic could lose reasoning context.
  • Test sketch: TestReviseThought_CreatesRevision — add a thought, revise it, verify revision has is_revision=true and revises_thought_id points to original.

docs.title: Gap #6: store/memories.go — Project chunk operations (5.5% coverage)

• Package: memory/search

  • Why it matters: The RRF fusion is the core of the search quality. A bug here produces wrong search results.
  • Test sketch: TestRRFFuseMultiList_ThreeLists — create 3 ranked lists with overlapping items, verify RRF scores are computed correctly and items are sorted by descending score.

  • Why it matters: This is the core orchestrator logic for building agent context packages. A bug here sends wrong context to agents.

  • Why it matters: This is the entry point for all TUI-to-backend communication. A bug here breaks the entire system.

docs.title: Session 23 — Reviewer Report (T-062)
docs.text: Date: 2026-06-05 Reviewer: neuralgentics-tester (deepseek-v4-flash) Baseline: neuralgentics v0.1.0, 131 .go files (60 *_test.go), 54 .ts source files + 21 test files Architect Input: docs/design/session-23-architect-review.md (585 lines)

docs.title: #1 (CRITICAL) — Broker.DeregisterServer kills shared proxy for ALL servers
docs.text:
• File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go:206-216
  • Problem: DeregisterServer calls b.proxy.Stop() which closes the single shared MCPProxy's reader channel and all pending response channels. Since NewBroker() creates exactly one MCPProxy (line 54), stopping it kills the async reader for every server, not just the one being deregistered. ReloadServer (line 146) and ReloadServerWithConfig (line 188) have the same issue.
  • Fix: Per-server proxy tracking. Each StartServer should create a dedicated reader goroutine; DeregisterServer stops only that server's reader. Alternatively, use a reference-counted reader that only stops when the last server deregisters.
  • Test: Add TestBroker_DeregisterServer_DoesNotStopOtherProxies — register two servers, start both, deregister one, verify the other can still receive calls.

docs.title: #2 (CRITICAL) — Broker reload test hangs indefinitely (30s timeout)
docs.text:
• File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/reload_test.go:102
  • Problem: TestReloadServer_RunningServer hangs for 30 seconds and times out. Stack trace shows goroutine stuck in proxy.go:104 (readLoop scanner). The test starts a real subprocess (e.g., cat or sleep), and when the process exits, the scanner blocks on stdout.Read() because the pipe is not closed. The Stop() method closes readerCh but the scanner is blocked on Read(), not on the channel select.
  • Fix: The readLoop at proxy.go:100 uses a non-blocking select on readerCh (line 106-110) but the scanner.Scan() at line 104 blocks the goroutine. When the subprocess exits, scanner.Scan() returns false (EOF), but if the pipe is never closed (e.g., subprocess hangs), the scanner blocks forever. Fix: use io.Copy with a context or set a read deadline on the pipe. Alternatively, close the stdout pipe explicitly in Stop().
  • Test: Already exists — just needs the fix to pass.

docs.title: #3 (HIGH) — GetMemory passes includeArchived bool as SQL parameter incorrectly
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories.go:94-99
  • Problem: When includeArchived=false, the code passes includeArchived (a bool) as the second query parameter to GetMemoryByID. The query at queries.go:67 is WHERE id = $1 AND ($2::boolean OR is_archived = FALSE). When $2=false, this becomes WHERE id = $1 AND (false OR is_archived = FALSE) which is correct. However, when includeArchived=true, the code uses GetMemoryByIDIncludeArchived (no $2). This is actually correct — the code path is correct for both cases. RETRACTED — the architect flagged this but it's not a bug. The code correctly branches on includeArchived.
  • Verdict: FALSE ALARM — code is correct.

docs.title: #4 (HIGH) — Silent error swallowing in 7+ Scan loops
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories.go:272,314,381,404,439,1452,1479
  • Problem: Row scanning errors use continue instead of logging or returning. Malformed database rows are silently dropped, making debugging impossible. Affected methods: GetRelationships, GetSupersessionChain, GetEntitiesByType, SearchEntities, GetEntityRelationships, SearchChunks, GetFileChunksByPath.
  • Fix: Replace continue with at minimum slog.Warn("scan error", "err", err) before continue. For critical paths, return the error.
  • Test: Add TestGetRelationships_MalformedRow — insert a row with NULL in a non-nullable column, verify the error is logged (not silently dropped).

docs.title: #5 (HIGH) — store package has 5.5% test coverage
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/ (memories.go, postgres.go, queries.go, search.go, agent_tools.go)
  • Problem: The store package is the largest and most critical package (1,768-line god-file + 378-line postgres.go + 653-line queries.go) but has only 5.5% statement coverage. The search package is similarly low at 26.8%. These packages contain all database CRUD operations, relationships, entities, peers, thought chains, audit, trust, project chunks, and 1024-dim operations.
  • Fix: Add unit tests with mock pgx.Rows and pgx.Row interfaces. At minimum, test AddMemory, GetMemory, CountMemories, CreateRelationship, UpsertEntity, AddPeer, StartThoughtChain, LogAuditEvent, AddProjectChunk, SearchChunks.
  • Test: See PART B — Coverage Audit for specific test recommendations.

docs.title: #6 (HIGH) — context_builder.go has zero tests
docs.text:
• File: packages/orchestrator-go/src/neuralgentics/orchestrator/context_builder.go (495 lines)
  • Problem: BuildContextPackage, StoreContextPackage, FetchContextPackage, StoreAgentWrapUp, FetchAgentWrapUp — 5 exported functions, 0 tests. This is the core orchestrator logic for building agent context packages.
  • Fix: Add context_builder_test.go with mock MemoryProvider. Test BuildContextPackage with various task types, test StoreContextPackage round-trip, test error paths (memory query failure, marshal failure).
  • Test: TestBuildContextPackage_WithRelevantMemories, TestStoreContextPackage_RoundTrip, TestBuildContextPackage_EmptyMemories.

docs.title: #7 (HIGH) — Broker Call has no timeout
docs.text:
• File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go:299-330
  • Problem: Call method has no context or timeout parameter. A hung MCP server blocks the broker indefinitely. The underlying proxy.sendRPC has a 30-second timeout (proxy.go:339), but the broker's Call doesn't expose this to callers or allow cancellation.
  • Fix: Add ctx context.Context parameter to Call. Use select with ctx.Done() in sendRPC. This is a breaking API change but necessary for production reliability.
  • Test: TestBroker_Call_ContextCancellation — cancel context before call completes, verify error.

docs.title: #8 (MEDIUM) — CountMemories broken primary query
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories.go:146
  • Problem: QueryRow(ctx, GetMemoryCount).Scan(nil, &active, nil) — scans into nil for two of three columns. The primary query GetMemoryCount returns 3 columns but only 1 is scanned. The fallback raw SQL at line 149 works correctly.
  • Fix: Fix GetMemoryCount SQL to return only 1 column, or fix the Scan to match the query columns.
  • Test: TestCountMemories_PrimaryQuery — verify the primary query path works (not just the fallback).

docs.title: #9 (MEDIUM) — Stub methods return nil,nil (indistinguishable from "no results")
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories.go:1302-1304 (GetTrustAdjustments), 1320-1322 (ListFadingMemories)
  • Problem: Both methods return nil, nil with // TODO comments. Callers cannot distinguish "no results" from "not implemented". The ListFadingMemories signature includes threshold float64 and limit int parameters that are completely ignored.
  • Fix: Either implement the queries or return fmt.Errorf("not implemented").
  • Test: TestGetTrustAdjustments_NotImplemented — verify error is returned, not nil.

docs.title: #10 (MEDIUM) — Security summary uses string-cast hours parameter
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories.go:1705
  • Problem: hoursStr := fmt.Sprintf("%d", hours) converts the integer to a string and passes it as a SQL argument. The queries use $1::integer cast, so PostgreSQL will cast it back, but this is fragile and non-idiomatic. Should pass hours directly as int.
  • Fix: Pass hours as int directly. The SQL queries use $1::integer which accepts both int and string parameters, but passing a string is misleading.
  • Test: TestGetSecuritySummary_HoursParameter — verify the query executes with integer parameter.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories.go (1,768 LOC)
docs.text: God-file with 61 methods spanning 10+ domains. Contains memory CRUD, relationships, entities, peers, thought chains, audit, trust/decay, project chunks, 1024-dim ops, user profiles, and security summaries. 7 silent error swallowing sites (rows.Scan errors continued). 2 stub methods returning nil,nil. 1 broken primary query (CountMemories). 1 non-idiomatic SQL parameter (hoursStr). The GetMemory branching logic is correct (verified). 5.5% coverage — the most critical coverage gap in the entire codebase.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/memory.go (670 LOC)
docs.text: Clean facade pattern. Well-structured with clear section headers. Dual-write 1024-dim sidecar in AddMemory is best-effort with proper logging. IndexProject background goroutine correctly uses jobsMu mutex. No bugs found. Coverage: ~85% (via integration tests).

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/postgres.go (378 LOC)
docs.text: Clean initialization with production-tuned pool settings (MinConns=4, MaxConns=25). Migration runner uses embedded FS. scanMemoryEntry and scanMemoryEntryWithDistance are well-structured. parseVectorString is correct. No dedicated tests — covered only via integration tests.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/queries.go (653 LOC)
docs.text: SQL constants only. No logic to test. Well-organized with clear section headers. One concern: GetMemoryByID at line 67 uses $2::boolean OR is_archived = FALSE — the $2 parameter is the includeArchived bool. When false, the query correctly filters to non-archived. When true, the code uses GetMemoryByIDIncludeArchived instead. Correct.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/index/indexer.go (248 LOC)
docs.text: Clean implementation with atomic.Bool for concurrent indexing protection. Batch processing with configurable batch size. Context cancellation respected. The addChunkWithVector method at line 197-206 is a workaround for ChunkResult not having a Vector field — the vector is silently dropped. Coverage: 85.9% (but the concurrent indexing test fails).

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/index/indexer_test.go (646 LOC)
docs.text: Mock store with 20+ stub methods for interface compliance. The TestProjectIndexer_Index_ConcurrentIndexing test at line 537 fails because the mock is too fast — the first indexing completes before the 50ms sleep finishes. The atomic.Bool works correctly; the test timing is wrong.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/search/hybrid.go (331 LOC)
docs.text: Well-implemented dual-model RRF search. Three modes (cpu/auto/gpu) with proper fallback. rrfFuseMultiList correctly handles dedup and sorting. HybridSearch has proper error handling (if one search fails, returns the other). Coverage: 26.8% — the RRF fusion logic is untested.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/dialectic/engine.go (350 LOC)
docs.text: Clean implementation. FindContradictions does explicit CONTRADICTS relationship search + implicit text similarity. Proper dedup with seen map. Best-effort implicit search (errors don't fail the whole operation). Coverage: 85.0%.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/trust/engine.go (126 LOC)
docs.text: Small, focused, well-tested. AdjustTrust correctly clamps to [0.0, 1.0], updates store, and logs adjustment. Coverage: 80.0%.

docs.title: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/kg/queries.go (335 LOC)
docs.text: BFS-based inference chain finding with cycle detection. Query dispatches to findInferenceChain or transitiveClosure based on whether EndEntity is set. Coverage: 78.6%.

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go (427 LOC)
docs.text: Clean facade with 20+ methods. Critical bug: DeregisterServer (line 210), ReloadServer (line 146), ReloadServerWithConfig (line 188) all call b.proxy.Stop() which kills the shared proxy for ALL servers. High: Call has no context/timeout parameter. Medium: MatchIntent creates a new IntentMatcher on every call (line 353). Coverage: ~55% (main broker package).

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/proxy/proxy.go (345 LOC)
docs.text: Async reader goroutine with channel-based response routing. sendRPC has 30s timeout. Stop() correctly closes all pending channels. Bug: readLoop at line 104 blocks on scanner.Scan() — the non-blocking select on readerCh at line 106-110 only works between scan iterations, not during a blocking read. If the subprocess hangs, the scanner blocks forever. Coverage: 78.8%.

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/access/access.go (271 LOC)
docs.text: Clean access control with role-based permissions. Coverage: 100%.

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/catalog/catalog.go (198 LOC)
docs.text: Server catalog builder with role filtering. Coverage: 83.9%.

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/launcher/launcher.go (195 LOC)
docs.text: Subprocess lifecycle management. Coverage: 67.2%.

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/intent/matcher.go (279 LOC)
docs.text: Jaccard similarity intent matcher. Coverage: 96.4%.

docs.title: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/registry/registry.go (155 LOC)
docs.text: In-memory server registry with RWMutex. Coverage: 38.5% — low for a simple data structure.

docs.title: packages/orchestrator-go/src/neuralgentics/orchestrator/task_runner.go (638 LOC)
docs.text: Kahn's algorithm topological sort, parallel group computation, ready/blocked task detection. nowMillis returns hardcoded 0 (line 583-585). tasksToEntries hardcodes AgentCoder (line 592) even though dispatchGroup later calls ResolveAgent. Coverage: ~55% (via orchestrator package).

docs.title: packages/orchestrator-go/src/neuralgentics/orchestrator/context_builder.go (495 LOC)
docs.text: 5 exported functions, 0 tests. BuildContextPackage queries memories, builds trust scores, extracts decisions/files/snippets, resolves scope/output/error handling. StoreContextPackage marshals to JSON and stores in memory. FetchContextPackage round-trips. All untested.

docs.title: packages/orchestrator-go/src/neuralgentics/orchestrator/protocol.go (266 LOC)
docs.text: 8-step Boomerang Protocol state machine with RWMutex. Advance validates transitions. Enforce blocks in strict mode. CompleteProtocol advances through remaining steps. Well-implemented. No dedicated tests (covered by e2e tests which fail due to Docker unavailability).

docs.title: packages/orchestrator-go/src/neuralgentics/orchestrator/orchestrator.go (371 LOC)
docs.text: Top-level orchestrator with HandleTask, Route, Dispatch, CompleteTaskCycle. Uses mutex for state. Coverage: ~55% (via orchestrator package).

docs.title: packages/backend-go/cmd/backend/main.go (2,166 LOC)
docs.text: Thin JSON-RPC stdio server. Large but mostly boilerplate (JSON-RPC dispatch, type conversions). Coverage: 32.3% (backend package) / 100% (types package).

docs.title: packages/tui/src/__tests__/neuralgentics-client.test.ts (259 LOC)
docs.text: 11 tests fail — all in the NeuralgenticsClient unit test suite. The tests mock a subprocess but the neuralgentics-backend binary is either not available or not behaving as expected. The resolveBackendPath tests also fail because the binary path resolution depends on the test environment.

docs.title: Bug #1: TestProjectIndexer_Index_ConcurrentIndexing — CONFIRMED
docs.text:
• File: packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/index/indexer_test.go:537
  • Status: FAILS 3/3 runs
  • Root cause: The test starts indexing in a goroutine, sleeps 50ms, then tries a second concurrent call. The mock store is too fast (simple map insert with mutex) — the first indexing completes before the 50ms sleep. The atomic.Bool in indexer.go:22-23 works correctly; the test timing is wrong.
  • Fix: Add a synchronization point in the mock (e.g., a channel that blocks AddProjectChunk until the test signals readiness).
  • Adjacent issues: The mock store at indexer_test.go:18-24 has 20+ stub methods for interface compliance. These stubs (lines 622-646) implement GetUserProfile, UpsertUserProfile, GetSecuritySummary, RecordToolRequest, IncrementToolUse, GetAgentTools — all returning nil/nil. This is the same pattern as the production stubs.

docs.title: Bug #2: TUI test failures — CONFIRMED (11 failures)
docs.text:
• File: packages/tui/src/__tests__/neuralgentics-client.test.ts
  • Status: 11 tests fail consistently
  • Root cause: The NeuralgenticsClient tests spawn a subprocess and communicate via JSON-RPC over stdio. The tests fail because the subprocess (neuralgentics-backend) either doesn't exist in the test PATH or doesn't produce the expected JSON output. The resolveBackendPath tests fail because the binary path resolution depends on environment variables that may not be set in CI.
  • Fix: Use a mock subprocess (e.g., a script that echoes valid JSON-RPC responses) instead of the real binary. Or set NEURALGENTICS_BACKEND_PATH in the test environment.
  • Adjacent issues: setup.test.ts has 5 tests that all pass. The resolveBackendPath tests in neuralgentics-client.test.ts are the ones failing (2 of the 11).

docs.title: Bug #3: Broker.DeregisterServer stops all proxies — CONFIRMED
docs.text:
• File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go:206-216
  • Status: Verified by reading proxy.go:Stop() at line 167-182
  • Root cause: NewBroker() at line 54 creates a single MCPProxy instance. DeregisterServer at line 210 calls b.proxy.Stop() which closes p.readerCh (signals reader goroutine to exit) and closes all pending channels. After this, no RPC calls can be made through the proxy. ReloadServer (line 146) and ReloadServerWithConfig (line 188) have the same issue.
  • Fix: Per-server reader tracking. Each StartServer should create a dedicated reader goroutine scoped to that server's stdout pipe. DeregisterServer stops only that server's reader.
  • Adjacent issues: The TestReloadServer_RunningServer test at reload_test.go:102 hangs for 30 seconds and times out. Stack trace shows goroutine stuck in proxy.go:104 (readLoop scanner). This is a separate but related issue — the scanner blocks on stdout.Read() even after Stop() is called because the non-blocking select on readerCh only works between scan iterations.

docs.title: Fix #1: Fix Broker reload test hang (HIGH) — ~1 hour
docs.text:
• Dependency: None
  • Scope: proxy/proxy.go:100-155 — make readLoop interruptible during blocking reads
  • Approach: Use io.Copy with a context or set a read deadline on the pipe. When Stop() is called, close the stdout pipe to unblock the scanner.
  • Why first: This is blocking CI. The 30s timeout wastes developer time.

docs.title: Fix #2: Fix Broker.DeregisterServer proxy sharing (CRITICAL) — ~2 hours
docs.text:
• Dependency: Fix #1 (need working proxy before refactoring)
  • Scope: broker.go, proxy/proxy.go — per-server reader tracking
  • Approach: Add StartReader(name string, stdout io.Reader) and StopReader(name string) to proxy. Track readers in a map. DeregisterServer calls StopReader(name) instead of Stop().
  • Why second: This is a correctness bug that silently breaks multi-server deployments.

docs.title: Fix #3: Fix silent error swallowing in Scan loops (HIGH) — ~15 minutes
docs.text:
• Dependency: None
  • Scope: 7 sites in store/memories.go
  • Approach: Replace continue with slog.Warn("scan error", "err", err); continue
  • Why third: Quick win with immediate debugging improvement.

docs.title: Fix #4: Fix CountMemories broken primary query (MEDIUM) — ~15 minutes
docs.text:
• Dependency: None
  • Scope: store/memories.go:146
  • Approach: Fix GetMemoryCount SQL to return 1 column, or fix Scan to match 3 columns.
  • Why fourth: Quick correctness fix.

docs.title: Fix #5: Remove or implement stub methods (MEDIUM) — ~15 minutes
docs.text:
• Dependency: None
  • Scope: store/memories.go:1302-1304, 1320-1322
  • Approach: Return fmt.Errorf("not implemented") instead of nil, nil.
  • Why fifth: Prevents silent failures in production.

Report complete. 12 critical/high findings, 3 bug verifications, 5 recommended fixes in dependency order.

docs.title: Session 26 — Coverage Push: God-File Split + Untested Files + RRF Gap
docs.text: Date: 2026-06-05 Phase: Coverage Stabilization Theme: De-risk the 5.5%-coverage store/ package by splitting the 1,773-line god-file Total Estimated Effort: ~6 days (11 cards, 0.5–1 day each) Baseline: neuralgentics v0.1.0, commit e74b7f2, working tree clean

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The store/ package is the data backbone of neuralgentics — every memory operation, relationship, entity, peer, thought chain, audit event, project chunk, and security query goes through PostgresStore. Yet the package has 5.5% test coverage (Session 23 audit, docs/design/session-23-coverage-audit.md), and its heart is a 1,773-line god-file (memories.go) with 78 methods spanning 10+ domains. This is the #1 priority from the Session 23 architect review (docs/design/session-23-architect-review.md, §3, Ranked Refactor Backlog #1).

docs.title: Why Now
docs.text: Metric Current Target store/memories.go LOC 1,773 ~230 after splits (CRUD section only) store/ package coverage 5.5% ≥80% God-file domains in one file 10 1 (CRUD naturally remains) Untested store/ files with dedicated tests 2 (vector.go + partial memories) 6+ (all source files)

docs.title: Safety Property
docs.text: The PostgresStore struct is defined in postgres.go:24. Go allows methods on a type to be defined in any file in the same package. Every extraction is pure code movement — zero logic changes, zero signature changes, zero import changes (all files stay in package store). The only risk is merge conflicts if two extractions modify overlapping line ranges in memories.go simultaneously. See §Parallelization for the safe strategy.

docs.title: Caller Graph (verified grep)
docs.text: All callers access methods via m.store.MethodName() (through the memory.go facade) or s.store.MethodName() (in search/hybrid.go). No caller imports individual files; all access is through the *PostgresStore receiver. Splitting methods into separate files preserves the receiver and method names — zero caller changes needed.

docs.title: T-COV-001: Extract memories_1024.go — 1024-Dim Dual-Model Ops
docs.text: Field Value Files in scope store/memories.go → new store/memories_1024.go Lines to extract Lines 1529–1621 (93 lines): AddMemory1024, QueryMemories1024, GetMemory1024, CountMemories1024, DeleteMemory1024 Target file packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/memories_1024.go Test acceptance ≥5 new unit tests in store/memories_1024_test.go. Tests: TestAddMemory1024_Success, TestAddMemory1024_MissingMemory, TestQueryMemories1024_VectorSearch, TestCountMemories1024_Empty, TestDeleteMemory1024_Success. ≥80% coverage on extracted code. Depends on None (extracts from the bottom of the file — no dependency on any other extraction) Blocks Nothing Effort 0.5 day Risk LOW — self-contained at end of file, no internal cross-references from above Verification go build ./packages/memory/... && go test ./packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/ -run "1024" -count=1

docs.title: T-COV-002: Extract profiles_security.go — User Profiles + Security Summary
docs.text: Field Value Files in scope store/memories.go → new store/profiles_security.go Lines to extract Lines 1622–1773 (152 lines): GetUserProfile, UpsertUserProfile, GetSecuritySummary Target file packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/profiles_security.go Test acceptance ≥4 new unit tests in store/profiles_security_test.go. Tests: TestGetUserProfile_Existing, TestGetUserProfile_NotFound, TestUpsertUserProfile_Create, TestUpsertUserProfile_Update, TestGetSecuritySummary_ValidRange. ≥80% coverage on extracted code. docs.title: Dependency Graph
docs.text:
T-COV-001 (1024 ops, bottom)
  └── T-COV-002 (profiles+security, above 1024)
        └── T-COV-003 (project chunks, above profiles)
              └── T-COV-004 (audit+trust, above chunks)
                    └── T-COV-005 (thoughts, above audit) [1 day]
                          └── T-COV-006 (peers, above thoughts)
                                └── T-COV-007 (entities, above peers)
                                      └── T-COV-008 (relationships, top section)
                                            ├── T-COV-009 (test postgres + agent_tools)
                                            ├── T-COV-010 (test search.go)
                                            └── T-COV-011 (RRF coverage)

Why sequential for T-COV-001 through T-COV-008: Each extraction removes lines from memories.go and adds them to a new file. Removing lines shifts all line numbers above. Extracting bottom-first means each card's line references in memories.go remain stable. Parallel extraction of non-overlapping regions is possible (see §Parallelization) but adds merge-conflict risk.

T-COV-009, T-COV-010, T-COV-011 are parallelizable — they touch different files, no shared state.

docs.title: Batch 1: Bottom + Top (2 parallel extractions, if safe)
docs.text:
Card Region Lines Rationale T-COV-001 Bottom (1529–1621) 93 Extracts from end of file; removing these doesn't shift anything above T-COV-008 Top (230–335) 106 Extracts from upper-middle; doesn't overlap with bottom region

Constraint: After Batch 1, memories.go has two "holes" — lines 230–335 and 1529–1621 are removed. Remaining contiguous regions: 1–229 (CRUD), 336–1528 (entities through project chunks), 1622–1773 (profiles). The line numbers for the middle region shift by -106 (relationship extraction above them). This is manageable but requires the Batch 1 coder to document the new line offsets.

Risk with Batch 1 parallel: If T-COV-001 and T-COV-008 both modify memories.go in parallel, git may not merge cleanly (both remove lines from the same file). Recommendation: Run them sequentially unless both coders coordinate through a shared intermediate commit.

docs.title: Batch 2: Sequential Middle Sweep (6 sequential extractions)
docs.text: After Batch 1, extract remaining domains bottom-to-top: 1. T-COV-002 (profiles — now at ~1622-end of remaining file) 2. T-COV-003 (project chunks) 3. T-COV-004 (audit+trust) 4. T-COV-005 (thoughts — largest, 1 day) 5. T-COV-006 (peers) 6. T-COV-007 (entities)

docs.title: Batch 3: Parallel Test Cards (3 parallel)
docs.text: After all extractions, all 3 test cards can run simultaneously: - T-COV-009 (postgres + agent_tools tests) - T-COV-010 (search.go tests) - T-COV-011 (RRF coverage in hybrid.go)

docs.title: Risk Register
docs.text: # Risk Severity Mitigation 1 Merge conflicts on memories.go from parallel extractions MEDIUM Use sequential extraction (bottom-to-top) or coordinate through shared intermediate commits. Each card commits its extraction before the next card starts. 2 Helper functions (scanThoughtRow, derefString, formatVector) lose accessibility LOW All helpers are package-level (unexported, lowercase first letter). They remain accessible from any file in package store. No change needed. If formatVector is needed by both project_chunks.go and memories_1024.go, move it to a shared store/vector_util.go. 3 InferenceChain BFS traversal breaks on extraction LOW Pure code movement — BFS algorithm doesn't change. However, the existing code was untested before extraction; any pre-existing BFS bug becomes visible when tests are added. 4 GetTrustAdjustments + ListFadingMemories stub methods LOW These already return fmt.Errorf("not implemented"). The Session 23 fix (replacing nil,nil with error returns) is already applied. Tests should assert the error is returned. 5 CountMemories broken primary query LOW Session 23 architect smell S5: QueryRow(ctx, GetMemoryCount).Scan(nil, &active, nil) scans into nil columns. The fallback raw SQL works. This is a pre-existing bug, not introduced by extraction. Fix inline during T-COV-008 or add a follow-up card. 6 Silent error swallowing in 7+ scan loops MEDIUM Session 23 architect smell S2: if err := rows.Scan(...); err != nil { continue } in relationships.go, entities.go, peers.go, project_chunks.go. The extraction cards do NOT change logic, so this smell persists. Covered by T-COV-008 acceptance test TestGetRelationships_MalformedRow — but the fix itself (changing continue to error propagation) should be in a separate bugfix card. 7 Testcontainers dependency for DB-backed tests MEDIUM Many tests (search.go, postgres.go, thought chain tests) need a pgx pool. The project uses testcontainers-go; ensure CI has Docker available. Alternatively, use mockpgx for lighter tests. 8 Existing test fixtures (memories_count_test.go, memories_get_test.go) reference PostgresStore methods LOW These test files already import store and call methods directly. After extraction, the methods are still on PostgresStore and still in package store. No fixture changes needed.
